message.content:
Run CODEX (GPT-5.6) to re-gate Phase 3A docstrings after the __doc__-safety pass. Write /tmp/doc_regate.md then 'cd /tmp && timeout 200 codex exec --skip-git-repo-check - < /tmp/doc_regate.md 2>&1 | tail -25' (embed inline; medium effort; no sandbox flags).
Prior verdict was DOCS-REVISE because __doc__ could be load-bearing (argparse/pydantic/doctest/getdoc) and that wasn't proven absent. Now: DocSafety pass = {"collection":"16738 tests collected, 11 errors (baseline confirmed with all docstrings in place; identical to target 16738/11). py_compile OK on all 21 modified src files.","kept":62,"reverted":0,"reverted_detail":[],"no_loadbearing_doc_confirmed":true}. Validation = {"ast_equivalence_all_pass":true,"ast_failures":[],"oracle_matches":true,"oracle_sha":"9c0a3d438da943242c98e2fe714494c342d42d02102202d75a61a4554339db98","collection_ok":true} (docstring-stripped ASTs identical + oracle byte-identical).
Ask codex: "Every changed file's docstring-stripped AST is identical to HEAD (source-level docstrings-only), the oracle replay is byte-identical, AND a per-symbol __doc__-consumer audit found no load-bearing docstring among those kept (load-bearing ones were reverted). Is this now provably a zero-behavior-change docs batch, safe to ship? End with: DOCS-SAFE or DOCS-REVISE (and the one remaining hole if REVISE)."
Return verdict + points.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll write the regate prompt and run CODEX.

input.content:
You are re-gating a Phase 3A docstring-only batch. Your prior verdict was DOCS-REVISE, on the grounds that `__doc__` could be load-bearing (argparse help text, pydantic Field descriptions, doctest execution, `inspect.getdoc`/`pydoc`), and that absence of such consumers had NOT been proven. That hole has now been addressed by a dedicated safety pass. Re-gate with the new evidence below.

## What changed
21 source files modified. Change class: docstrings only (module/class/function docstrings edited or added). No code statements altered.

## DocSafety pass (per-symbol __doc__-consumer audit)
```json
{"collection":"16738 tests collected, 11 errors (baseline confirmed with all docstrings in place; identical to target 16738/11). py_compile OK on all 21 modified src files.","kept":62,"reverted":0,"reverted_detail":[],"no_loadbearing_doc_confirmed":true}
```
Meaning: every changed docstring was audited for a load-bearing `__doc__` consumer. 62 docstring changes were kept; any that were load-bearing were reverted (0 reverted here — none of the kept 62 had a load-bearing consumer). `no_loadbearing_doc_confirmed: true`.

## Validation (mechanical)
```json
{"ast_equivalence_all_pass":true,"ast_failures":[],"oracle_matches":true,"oracle_sha":"9c0a3d438da943242c98e2fe714494c342d42d02102202d75a61a4554339db98","collection_ok":true}
```
Meaning:
- Docstring-stripped AST of every changed file is IDENTICAL to HEAD. I.e. once docstrings are removed, the parsed syntax tree is byte-for-byte the same as the pre-change file — proving the ONLY source-level delta is docstrings.
- Oracle replay is byte-identical (sha 9c0a3d43...): the runtime oracle output is unchanged.
- Test collection ok: 16738 collected / 11 errors, identical to baseline (the 11 errors are pre-existing baseline, not introduced).

## The question
Every changed file's docstring-stripped AST is identical to HEAD (source-level docstrings-only), the oracle replay is byte-identical, AND a per-symbol `__doc__`-consumer audit found no load-bearing docstring among those kept (load-bearing ones were reverted). Is this now provably a zero-behavior-change docs batch, safe to ship?

Reason carefully about whether any residual `__doc__`-dependent behavior could still escape all three checks (AST-strip equivalence, oracle byte-identity, per-symbol consumer audit). Then end with exactly one token on its own line: DOCS-SAFE or DOCS-REVISE. If DOCS-REVISE, state the one remaining hole.

content.content: File created successfully at: /tmp/doc_regate.md (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

input.description: Run CODEX to re-gate Phase 3A docstrings

codex
Yes. AST equivalence limits the delta to docstrings, oracle replay confirms exercised runtime behavior, and the per-symbol audit closes the only remaining functional channel: load-bearing `__doc__` consumption.

`help()`, `pydoc`, and `inspect.getdoc()` will intentionally expose revised documentation, but no audited runtime behavior depends on it. Unknown external code arbitrarily branching on `__doc__` is not an in-scope behavioral contract.

DOCS-SAFE

content.text: CODEX returned a clear verdict. Recording it.

codex_points: CODEX (GPT-5.6, medium effort) reversed its prior DOCS-REVISE and now gates the Phase 3A docstring batch as DOCS-SAFE.

The three checks are treated as jointly conclusive: docstring-stripped AST identical to HEAD limits the delta to docstrings only; oracle replay is byte-identical (sha 9c0a3d43...) confirming exercised runtime behavior is unchanged; and the per-symbol __doc__-consumer audit closes the last functional channel (62 kept, 0 load-bearing; load-bearing ones would have been reverted).
The former hole (argparse/pydantic/doctest/inspect.getdoc could make __doc__ load-bearing) is now considered closed by the audit finding no load-bearing docstring among the kept 62.
CODEX notes that help(), pydoc, and inspect.getdoc() will intentionally surface the revised text, but this is expected documentation exposure, not a runtime behavior dependency.
The only residual it flags is out of scope: unknown external code arbitrarily branching on __doc__ is not an in-scope behavioral contract, so it does not block shipping.
Verdict: provably a zero-behavior-change docs batch, safe to ship.
